title: The BrightPath Outage: Students Locked Out

narrator.personality: Junior SRE on their first solo on-call shift. Nervous but thorough. Keeps mentioning that students have midterm deadlines tomorrow.

story_beats.message: Support inbox just hit 40 tickets. A professor tweeted that BrightPath is down during midterm week. The CEO is asking for a status update.
Engineering lead just joined the call. She says the last deployment was a security hardening sprint -- they tightened firewall rules across all environments.
That is not matching what we see. The instance itself is running fine -- this looks like a network access issue. Check what traffic is allowed to reach the instance.

glossary.Security Group: A virtual firewall that controls inbound and outbound traffic for an EC2 instance. It works at the instance level and only has allow rules -- if traffic is not explicitly allowed, it is silently dropped.
glossary.Inbound Rule: A security group rule that defines what incoming traffic is permitted to reach the instance. Each rule specifies a protocol, port range, and source (such as an IP range or another security group).
glossary.CIDR Block: A notation like 0.0.0.0/0 that represents a range of IP addresses. 0.0.0.0/0 means all IPv4 addresses on the internet.
glossary.Port 443: The standard network port for HTTPS traffic. Web browsers use this port when connecting to sites over a secure (TLS/SSL) connection.
glossary.Default Deny: The behavior where all traffic is blocked unless a rule explicitly allows it. Security groups use default deny for inbound traffic -- no rule means no access.
glossary.Network ACL: A stateless firewall that operates at the subnet level, one layer above the security group. It has both allow and deny rules and evaluates them in order by rule number.
glossary.Stateful Firewall: A firewall that remembers active connections. If an inbound rule allows a request in, the response is automatically allowed out without needing a separate outbound rule.
glossary.VPC: Virtual Private Cloud -- an isolated virtual network in AWS where you launch resources like EC2 instances. It has its own IP address range, subnets, and routing.
glossary.Internet Gateway: A VPC component that allows communication between instances in a public subnet and the internet. Without it, no internet traffic can reach the VPC.
glossary.Health Check: An automated test that verifies whether an application or instance is responding correctly. A failing health check does not always mean the instance is broken -- it could mean traffic cannot reach it.

narrative_arc.call: A PagerDuty alert fires at 9:47 PM. The BrightPath learning platform is not responding. 3,400 students have midterm papers due in ten hours.
narrative_arc.threshold: The on-call engineer opens the incident channel and crosses from normal operations into the unknown. The site is unreachable but the instance is running. The usual explanations do not apply.
narrative_arc.trials: The engineer checks instance status, application logs, database connections, CPU metrics. Everything reads healthy. Traffic metrics show packets dropping to zero at 19:42 UTC. The problem is invisible from the inside.
narrative_arc.revelation: The security group sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 has no inbound rule for port 443. The security hardening sprint two hours earlier removed it. The firewall has been silently dropping every student connection since 19:42.
narrative_arc.return: One rule is added back. Traffic restores in seconds. The post-incident review produces two new requirements: automated validation of security group changes against required ports, and staging tests before production changes.

components.name: Internet Gateway (igw-0f1e2d3c4b5a)
components.role: Routes inbound HTTPS traffic from students on the public internet into the VPC
components.failure_impact: If removed or detached, no internet traffic reaches the VPC at all

components.name: Public Subnet (10.0.1.0/24)
components.role: Hosts the EC2 web server in an internet-accessible subnet within the VPC
components.failure_impact: If route table lacks a default route to the internet gateway, outbound and inbound internet traffic stops

components.name: Network ACL (acl-0fedcba987654321)
components.role: Stateless subnet-level firewall that evaluates inbound and outbound rules by rule number
components.failure_impact: If a deny rule for port 443 is added above the allow rule, traffic is blocked at the subnet boundary

components.name: Security Group (sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890)
components.role: Stateful instance-level firewall that controls which traffic can reach the EC2 instance
components.failure_impact: If the inbound rule for port 443 is missing, all HTTPS traffic is silently dropped before reaching the application

components.name: EC2 Instance (brightpath-prod-web-01)
components.role: Runs the BrightPath LMS application on port 443, serving student traffic
components.failure_impact: If the instance stops or the application crashes, health checks fail and no requests are served

components.name: RDS MySQL (brightpath-db)
components.role: Stores course materials, student submissions, and user accounts
components.failure_impact: If the database is unreachable, the application returns errors but the site still loads partially

system_narration.data_flow: Students send HTTPS requests to the public IP 54.210.128.33. Traffic enters the VPC through the Internet Gateway, passes through the Network ACL at the subnet boundary, then through the Security Group attached to the EC2 instance. The application on port 443 processes the request, queries RDS over the private subnet, and returns the response back through the same path.
system_narration.what_broke: At 19:42 UTC, the inbound rule allowing TCP 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 was removed from security group sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 during a security hardening sprint. The security group reverted to default-deny for HTTPS. Every student request was silently dropped at the security group boundary. The instance, application, and database continued running normally -- the break was purely at the network access layer.

hints.text: The application is running on the instance. Can you check what network rules control access to it?
Security groups act as virtual firewalls for EC2 instances. What inbound rules are configured?
Look at the inbound rules -- is port 443 allowed? The application serves HTTPS traffic on that port.
The security group has no inbound rule for port 443. That means all HTTPS traffic from users is being dropped at the network level.

resolution.root_cause: The EC2 instance security group sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 was modified during a security hardening sprint, removing the inbound rule for port 443 (HTTPS). All user traffic to the BrightPath learning platform is dropped at the security group level.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the security group is missing an inbound rule for port 443 (HTTPS)
Player connects the missing rule to the recent security hardening deployment
Player proposes adding an inbound rule allowing TCP port 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 (or appropriate CIDR) to the security group

learning_objectives: Security groups are stateful firewalls that control inbound and outbound traffic to EC2 instances
Security group rules are deny-by-default -- if no rule explicitly allows traffic, it is dropped
Changes to security groups take effect immediately with no downtime or restart required
sop_steps: 1. Acknowledge the PagerDuty alert and join the BrightPath incident channel.
2. Confirm the instance brightpath-prod-web-01 (i-0abc123def456789a) is in a running state using describe-instances.
3. Check application-level health by reviewing CloudWatch logs for brightpath-lms -- confirm the app is healthy but receiving no traffic.
4. Review CloudWatch NetworkPacketsIn metric for i-0abc123def456789a -- confirm traffic dropped to near-zero at approximately 19:42 UTC.
5. Describe the security group sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890 and list all inbound rules using describe-security-groups.
6. Identify that no inbound rule exists for TCP port 443 (HTTPS) from 0.0.0.0/0.
7. Cross-check the Network ACL acl-0fedcba987654321 to confirm it allows TCP 443 inbound -- rule the NACL out as the cause.
8. Add an inbound rule to sg-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890: protocol TCP, port 443, source 0.0.0.0/0 and ::/0 for IPv6.
9. Verify the fix by confirming the /health endpoint responds from an external network and CloudWatch health check alarm returns to OK.
10. Document the incident timeline, root cause, and remediation in the post-incident review channel.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Network ACL deny rule blocks port 443
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of the security group, a Network ACL deny rule with a lower rule number than the allow rule blocks HTTPS traffic at the subnet level. Because NACLs are stateless, both inbound and outbound rules must be checked. The security group would look correct in this case, making the problem harder to spot.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Audit NACL rules after changes and ensure allow rules for required ports have lower rule numbers than any broad deny rules. Use VPC Flow Logs to confirm where traffic is being rejected.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Route table missing default route to Internet Gateway
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The public subnet route table loses its 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing to the Internet Gateway. All internet traffic stops reaching the subnet entirely -- not just one port. The instance also loses outbound internet access, which distinguishes it from a security group issue.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Tag production route tables and use AWS Config rules to detect when a public subnet route table lacks a default route to an Internet Gateway.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Elastic IP or public IP disassociated from instance
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The instance loses its public IP address, so DNS resolution or direct IP access fails. Unlike a security group issue, the instance becomes unreachable on all ports, and the public IP no longer appears in describe-instances output.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Use Elastic IPs for production instances instead of auto-assigned public IPs. Set up CloudWatch alarms to alert when a production instance has no associated public IP.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Security group connection tracking limit exceeded
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The security group rules are correct, but the instance has too many concurrent tracked connections and starts dropping new ones. Symptoms appear as intermittent timeouts under high load rather than a complete outage. ENA driver metrics show conntrack_allowance_exceeded incrementing.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Monitor conntrack_allowance_available via ENA driver metrics. Scale to a larger instance type for higher connection tracking limits, or configure security group rules to avoid tracking where possible.

sop_practices: Always validate security group rules against a documented list of required application ports before and after any modification -- automate this check in CI/CD pipelines.
Test security group changes in a staging environment that mirrors production network topology before applying them to production.
Enable VPC Flow Logs on production subnets to provide an audit trail of accepted and rejected traffic, which accelerates root-cause analysis during connectivity incidents.
Set up CloudWatch alarms on external reachability probes (not just instance status checks) so that network-level outages are detected within minutes, not hours.
